coredata.dc:title: Band structure induced photodegradation mechanism of PEI-based carbon quantum dots for tetracycline and toxicity evaluation for these intermediates
coredata.dc:creator: Guo, Yong
Fei, Ziying
Chen, Ting
Guo, Ying
Xia, Yin
coredata.dc:description: Many kinds of carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are already released into the environment, and the factor that determine the photodegradation ability of these CQDs for antibiotics is still unclear. Herein, three PEI-based CQDs (CAPEI, HAPEI and BCNPEI) were prepared by hydrothermal method to clarify the structural factors that determine their photodegradation performance against tetracycline (TC) when exposed to visible light. Experimentally, BCNPEI photodegraded 62 % of TC, while CAPEI and HAPEI photodegraded TC only about 5–8 %. Sizes, functional groups, aromaticity and energy band structures of these CQDs have been analyzed, and the energy band structure has the best linear relationship with the photodegradation properties of CQDs on TC. The conduction band (CB) value of BCNPEI is − 0.623 V and the valence band (VB) value is 2.207 V, which are strong enough to produce hydroxyl radicals (·OH) and superoxide radicals (·O2 -) under visible light exposure. And this has been verified with photoactive species capture experiments and ESR experiments. Thus, band structure is the factor that determine the photocatalytic performance of CQDs for TC. Furthermore, a possible photodegradation pathway of TC by CQD was proposed in a study of high-performance liquid chromatography-mass spectrometry (HPLC-MS) results. The photodegradation intermediates of TC were less toxic than TC, suggesting that the photodegradation of TC by CQD can reduce its environmental risk. In this study, valuable insights are provided into the behavior of CQDs exposed to the environment.

TEM images and particle size distribution of (a) CAPEI, (b) HAPEI and (c) BCNPEI. The scale is 20 nm.

(a) Comparison of XRD spectra among CAPEI, HAPEI and BCNPEI; (b) comparison of FTIR spectra among CAPEI, HAPEI and BCNPEI; (c) the C1s spectra of CAPEI, HAPEI and BCNPEI; (d) the B1s spectra of BCNPEI.

(a) The bandgaps of CAPEI, HAPEI and BCNPEI; Mott–Schottky plots of (b) CAPEI, (c) HAPEI and (d) BCNPEI based on flat band potential; (e) the band structures of CAPEI, HAPEI and BCNPEI; (f) the effect of TB, BQ and EDTA-2Na on TC removal by BCNPEI under irradiation with visible light.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Many kinds of carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are already released into the environment, and the factor that determine the photodegradation ability of these CQDs for antibiotics is still unclear. Herein, three PEI-based CQDs (CAPEI, HAPEI and BCNPEI) were prepared by hydrothermal method to clarify the structural factors that determine their photodegradation performance against tetracycline (TC) when exposed to visible light. Experimentally, BCNPEI photodegraded 62 % of TC, while CAPEI and HAPEI photodegraded TC only about 5–8 %. Sizes, functional groups, aromaticity and energy band structures of these CQDs have been analyzed, and the energy band structure has the best linear relationship with the photodegradation properties of CQDs on TC. The conduction band (CB) value of BCNPEI is − 0.623 V and the valence band (VB) value is 2.207 V, which are strong enough to produce hydroxyl radicals (·OH) and superoxide radicals (·O2 -) under visible light exposure. And this has been verified with photoactive species capture experiments and ESR experiments. Thus, band structure is the factor that determine the photocatalytic performance of CQDs for TC. Furthermore, a possible photodegradation pathway of TC by CQD was proposed in a study of high-performance liquid chromatography-mass spectrometry (HPLC-MS) results. The photodegradation intermediates of TC were less toxic than TC, suggesting that the photodegradation of TC by CQD can reduce its environmental risk. In this study, valuable insights are provided into the behavior of CQDs exposed to the environment.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The data that has been used is confidential.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) in general are carbon nanoparticles less than 10 nm in diameter [1]. Due to good electrical conductivity, low toxicity, unique optical and optoelectronic properties [2], they have been widely used in many fields, such as electrocatalysis [3–5], chemical probes [6–8], cellular imaging [9–11], photocatalysis [12–14], photodynamic therapy [15,16], etc. In the above fields, photocatalysis is a process using light energy to realize the photocatalytic hydrogen evolution and photodegradation of pollutants [17]. The wide application of CQDs in photocatalysis mainly focuses on combining CQDs with photocatalytic materials to improve its photocatalytic performance. Since CQDs have good semiconducting and upconverted luminescence properties, coupling them with photocatalysts can improve the activity of photocatalytic materials [18]. For example, CQD/TiO2 is prepared via hydrothermal method, which can effectively degrade methylene blue under simulated solar light irradiation [19]. CQDs/Ag3PO4 photocatalysts has better photodegradation performance for methyl orange and structural stability than Ag3PO4 [20]. ZnO foam/carbon quantum dot nanocomposites display outstanding photocatalytic ability for dye degradation under ultraviolet and visible light illumination [21].

However, the studies of CQDs alone as photocatalytic materials are still relatively few. CQDs synthesized via alkali-assisted electrochemical methods can photo-oxidize benzyl alcohol under the irradiation of near-infrared light to produce benzaldehyde [22]. N-doped carbon dots (NCDs) obtained from the one-pot sonication reaction of glucose and ammonium hydroxide shows excellent photodegradation of methyl orange when exposed to visible light [23]. The factors affecting their photodegradability of pollutants are not clear. Some research results have suggested that due to the high band bending caused by the CO and COOH groups in CQD, it can separate the photo-induced electron-hole pairs more efficiently and thus improve its own photocatalytic activity [24]. In addition, CQD photocatalytic performance has also been explained by band structure theory. The conduction band (CB) and valence band (VB) energies of CQDs are related to the formation of ·O2 − and ·OH, respectively [25–27]. So far, some CQDs can degrade organic pollution under light irradiation, whereas some cannot. The structural determinants of the photocatalytic activity of CQDs themselves remain unclear.

CQD exhibits excellent water dispersibility. In many previous studies, the powder photocatalysts are easily agglomerated in the matrix to prepare non-powder photocatalytic materials, such as photocatalytic coatings and hydrogels [28,29]. Therefore, the development of water-soluble photocatalytic materials and the elucidation of the mechanisms affecting their performance are helpful to solve the above agglomeration problem. Furthermore, CQD photocatalytic materials are promising for a wide range of applications in photodynamic therapy of diseased cells such as cancer [30,31]. The clarification of the factors that influence the photodegradation performance of CQDs is also advantageous for promoting their application in photodynamic therapy. Due to the wide application of CQDs and their hydrophilicity, the entry of CQDs into the environment is inevitable [32,33]. There have been many works on the toxicity of CQDs to aquatic organisms (e.g., algae) [34,35], but the effects of CQDs on the transformation of organic pollutants have not been reported yet. Clarifying these issues will provide further insight into the impact of CQDs on organic pollutants in the water body.

Herein, a series of PEI-based CQDs (including CAPEI, HAPEI and BCNPEI) have been synthesized. The size, functional group, aromaticity and band structure of CQDs were compared to elucidate the structural factors that determine their photodegradability of tetracycline (TC). Being a wide-used antibiotic, TC is often used to assess the photodegradation properties of photocatalytic materials [36–39]. The obtained results indicate that band structure makes main contribution for the photodegradation of these CQDs. The toxicity of TC is greatly diminished by the photo-degrading behavior of CQD, which may be helpful the further bio-degradation by these microorganism and plants in water. This work aims to achieve the following objectives: (1) clarifying the structural factors influencing the photodegradation performance of these PEI-based CQDs for TC; (2) providing clarification on the photodegradation pathway by BCNPEI to TC and the toxic changes of TC photodegradable intermediate agents.

Polyethyleneimine (PEI), citric acid (CA), hyaluronic acid (HA), diboron trioxide (B2O3), melamine (C3H6N6), tetracycline (TC), p-benzoquinone (BQ), tert-butanol (TB) and disodium ethylenediamine tetraacetate (EDTA-2Na).

Carbon-doped boron nitride (BCN) was prepared in the same manner as our previous work [40]: B2O3 and C3H6N6 in a molar ratio of 1:0.5 was added to deionized water and then dried at 90 °C after stirring well. The precursor material was calcined by muffle furnace (heating rate: 10 °C/min) at 1100 °C for 4 h. After the solid in the crucible cools naturally, it is rinsed with deionized water, and once the pH of the cleaning solution is neutral, it is dried at 90 °C to obtain the BCN powder (Fig. S1(a)).

CAPEI was synthesized with citric acid and polyethyleneimine as precursors: 0.3 g of CA was weighed and added into 60 mL of deionized water, then stirred until dissolved; after that, the CA solution was stirred continuously with 0.6 g of PEI added to ensure that the two substances were well mixed; the mixed solution was then transferred to stainless steel hydrothermal reactors, followed by 4 h at 180 °C in an oven. After cooling, the mixture was centrifuged at 5000 rpm for 15 min to remove the precipitate and obtain a supernatant. Unreacted precursors and other impurities generated were removed from the supernatant using a dialysis bag (1000 Kuda) with deionized water for 48 h. After freeze-drying the dialysate, a dark yellow powder was obtained, called CAPEI carbon quantum dot (Fig. S1(b)).
HAPEI (Fig. S1(c)) and BCNPEI (Fig. S1(d)) were prepared with the same procedure of CAPEI, except that CA was replaced by HA and BCN, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of CAPEI, HAPEI and BCNPEI
ce:section.ce:para: The morphologies of CAPEI, HAPEI and BCNPEI were studied by a Transmission Electron Microscope (TEM) (JEM2100F, Japan). Infrared spectrometry (Nicolet iS5 FT-IR instrument, USA) was applied to examine the Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra of CAPEI, HAPEI, and BCNPEI. The X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns of CAPEI, HAPEI and BCNPEI were obtained with an X-ray Diffractometer radiation (Bruker D8 ADVANCE, Germany). The X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) characterizations of CAPEI, HAPEI and BCNPEI were performed using an XPS spectrometer (Thermo ESCALAB 250Xi, USA). The UV–visible diffuse reflectance spectra (UV-DRS) of CAPEI, HAPEI and BCNPEI were measured using an UV–vis spectrophotometer (Agilent CARY 100, USA). The conduction band edges of CAPEI, HAPEI and BCNPEI were determined using an electrochemical workstation (CHI660, China). Electron spin resonance (ESR) was employed to detect active species for TC removal. The photodegradation intermediates related to TC were examined by high performance liquid chromatography-mass (HPLC-MS) spectrometer (Agilent 6530, Japan).

The interaction of CAPEI, HAPEI and BCNPEI with TC under dark condition was studied via UV–vis spectrophotometer. The specific experimental steps were as follows: 20 mg of CAPEI, HAPEI and BCNPEI were respectively added to TC solution (concentration: 20 mg/L); these mixed solutions were then stirred for 300 min under light-shielding condition; thereafter, the characteristic spectrum of CAPEI, HAPEI, BCNPEI, TC, CAPEI-TC, HAPEI-TC and BCNPEI-TC solutions were detected by UV–vis spectrophotometer, and then these characteristic spectra were compared to determine the interaction between these three CQDs and TC. To guarantee the accuracy of the experiments, three parallel experiments were performed simultaneously.

The performance of CAPEI, HAPEI, BCNPEI in degrading TC under visible light illumination was studied with the following procedure: 20 mg of sample was added to 100 mL of TC solution at a concentration of 20 mg/L and then for 300 min in the dark with stirring; subsequently, a xenon lamp with a 420 nm filter was used to irradiate the solution and 3 mL of the solution was taken each 30 min. The photodegradation experiment was terminated after 180 min of irradiation. These obtained solutions were characterized using a UV–vis spectrophotometer to determine the change of TC concentration during photodegradation process.
These taken solution samples were also characterized by HPLC-MS to identify the intermediate products of TC under irradiation with visible light by BCNPEI.

To identify photoactive species during TC photodegradation, radical trapping experiments were performed on the basis of the above-mentioned experimental procedures. After the dark reaction in the above process, three groups of experiments were set up on the sample solution: 0.0108 g BQ, 0.186 g EDTA- 2Na and 0.0371 g TB were added to the photodegradation systems to capture superoxide radicals (·O2 -), holes (h+) and hydroxyl radicals (·OH), separately [25].

The standard curve of TC solution is displayed in Fig. S2(a). The correlation coefficient R2 between the absorbance and concentration of the solution is significantly greater than 0.999, indicating an excellent linear relationship between the UV absorption peak and the concentration of TC.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photo-stability and recycle experiments of BCNPEI
ce:section.ce:para: The photo-stability of BCNPEI was investigated when exposed to visible light. Three 20 mg samples of BCNPEI powder were illuminated under xenon lamp (equipped with 420 nm filter) for 5 h, 10 h and 15 h, respectively. Then, the three samples were characterized by FTIR spectroscopy to evaluate the photo-stability of BCNPEI.

Next, 20 mg/L TC solution was prepared and its absorbance was measured by UV–vis spectrophotometer as a1. 20 mg BCNPEI was added to 100 mL TC solution (concentration of 20 mg/L) and stirred in the dark for 300 min. After complete adsorption, the solution was irradiated under a xenon lamp equipped with a 420 nm filter for 180 min, and the absorbance was measured as b1. Then, a minute amount of TC solution with a concentration of 200 mg/L was slowly added to the TC solution after one photodegradation with a dropper (try to ensure that the volume of the solution remains unchanged), and the absorbance was measured until the absorbance rose from b1 to a1. The solution was again illuminated with a xenon lamp for 180 min and the absorbance was measured and recorded as b2. The above steps were repeated twice and the absorbance obtained was recorded as b3 and b4.
All of the described experiments were performed in three parallel sets to guarantee that the results obtained were correct.

Based on the TEM, XPS and FTIR characterization results of CAPEI and HAPEI, the CQD model (Fig. S3(a)) was constructed with aromatic ring structure, CC single bonds, COH bonds, CC double bonds, CO double bonds, etc. The characterization results of BCNPEI show that BCNPEI has not only aromatic ring structure containing CC single bonds, COH bonds, CC double bonds and CO double bonds, but also B-N heterocyclic structure. So, the CQD model with doped BCN heterocyclic ring (BCN-CQD model) was constructed (Fig. S3(b)). To gain insight into the optical behavior of BCNPEI, the highest unoccupied molecular orbital (HOMO) and lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) of the CQD model and the BCN-CQD model were investigated in the Gaussian 09 procedure using the m062x/6-31g (d, p) method. And the interaction energy of BCN-CQD model with TC, degradation product 3 and degradation product 4 was calculated [25,41]. All of these models were constructed by GView 5.0 program.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Study on the Interaction and photodegradation of TC by PEI-based CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: To explore the interaction between these three PEI-based CQDs and TC under dark conditions, the UV-Vis spectra of CAPEI, HAPEI, BCNPI, and their mixed solutions with TC were studied.

TC has a distinct absorption peak near 358 nm, and the intensity of this peak is commonly used to determine the concentration of TC. As can be seen from Fig. 1(a), CAPEI has two obvious absorption peaks between 200 and 400 nm. Among them, the peak at 240 nm is due to the electronic leap from the π to π* orbital of the polycyclic aromatic ring structural domain in CAPEI, and the peak at 354 nm is attributed to the n-π* leap of the CN bond [42]. The UV absorption spectra of the CAPEI-TC solution at the start of mixing and after 300 min of stirring in the dark conditions both show an absorption peak at 354 nm, indicating that the absorbance of the CAPEI-TC mixed solution is a superposition of the two in the dark environment, with very weak interaction. HAPEI exhibits a distinctive absorption peak at 358 nm (Fig. 1(b)). The absorbance peak of the HAPEI-TC mixed solution was 358 nm and did not change significantly even after 300 min of stirring in the absence of light conditions, indicating that the interaction between HAPEI and TC is not obvious under dark conditions, as evidenced by the absorbance of the mixed solution behaving as a superposition of the two. As shown in Fig. 1(c), BCNPEI has a low-intensity UV absorption peak at 366 nm, whereas the BCNPEI-TC mixture displays a characteristic peak near 369 nm both at the start and after 300 min of stirring. This implies that there is an interaction between BCNPEI and TC in the darkened environment, and that the structure of the solute has changed, resulting in a red-shift in the spectrum.

Then, TC photodegradation by CAPEI, HAPEI and BCNPEI was carried out. According to the photodegradation experiment results (Fig. 1(d)), the removal rate of TC by BCNPEI reached 62 %, while the photodegradation effect of CAPEI and HAPEI on TC was very weak, only about 5–8 %. Moreover, CAPEI, HAPEI, and BCNPEI had few effects on the adsorption of TC in the light-free reaction phase, and their removal of TC was mainly concentrated in the photocatalytic stage. Meanwhile, the photostability of TC was investigated. It was found that the adsorption peak (358 nm) of TC was almost unchanged before and after light (Fig. S2(b)), indicating that TC has good stability under the exposure to visible light. Moreover, the kinetic curves of the photodegradation of TC by the three CQDs were fitted, and all of them conformed to the first-order kinetic model shown in Fig. S4. Among them, the optical degradation rate of BCNPEI for TC was 0.004741 min−1, and the optical degradation rates of CAPEI and HAPEI for TC were 0.000503 min−1 and 0.000327 min−1, respectively. The findings showed that BCNPEI had the highest efficiency in photodegradation of TC when exposed to visible light.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterizations of the PEI-based CQDs and the photodegradation mechanisms of these CQDs for TC
ce:section.ce:para: The three PEI-based CQDs have been characterized by TEM, XRD, FTIR and XPS methods. CAPEI, HAPEI and BCNPEI are all yellow powder samples, of which BCNPEI has the lightest color and HAPEI the darkest, and they can be evenly dispersed in water, forming light yellow solutions of varying shades (Fig. S1(b)–(d)).

CAPEI, HAPEI and BCNPEI were analyzed by TEM. In Fig. 2, the three CQDs are spherical, and the CAPEI particle size ranges from 2 to 10 nm, with an average particle size of about 7.39 nm. HAPEI particles range from 2 to 10 nm in size, with an average of 4.96 nm. The particle size of BCNPEI ranges from 2 nm to 6 nm, with an average of about 4.04 nm. No obvious agglomeration is found in the TEM images of the three CQDs, which is consistent with the experimental finding that PEI-based CQDs can be dispersed in water. There is a similar particle size between HAPEI and BCNPEI, both around 4 nm, while CAPEI has an average particle size about 1.83 times larger than the other two CQDs. Obviously, there is no relationship between particle size distribution and photodegradation properties.

Then, CAPEI, HAPEI and BCNPEI were characterized by XRD. As displayed in Fig. 3(a), all three CQDs have a broad characteristic peak around 22° which is assigned to the reflection from the (002) plane of CQDs [43–45]. And the characteristic diffraction peak at 42.9° is a (100) crystal plane unique to the BCN material in BCNPEI [46]. The XRD pattern shows that the carbon quantum dot material formed after high temperature hydrothermal treatment in this study has a good crystal structure. Although the crystal structures of CAPEI, HAPEI and BCNPEI are similar, their photocatalytic activities are different, indicating that the crystal structure is also not a dominant factor in the ability of CQDs to photo-degrade organic contaminants.

Previous studies have shown that surface functional groups affect the photocatalytic activity of CQDs [1,47]. Thus, FTIR and XPS characterization of CAPEI, HAPEI and BCNPEI were performed to study their surface functional groups. The FTIR spectra of CAPEI, HAPEI and BCNPEI are displayed in Fig. 3(b), where the characteristic peaks located at 3364 cm−1, 1622 cm−1, 1150 cm−1 and 1039 cm−1 belong to the stretching vibration of OH, CC, CC and CO bonds, respectively. The characteristic peaks at 2944 cm−1 and 2842 cm−1 are attributed to the CH bond [48]. Additionally, the characteristic peak of CAPEI at 1760 cm−1 corresponds to the CO bond, and the characteristic peaks of BCNPEI at 1316 cm−1 and 788 cm−1 correspond to the bending vibration of BNB bond and the stretching vibration of BN bond, respectively [49–51]. The FTIR spectra showed that they all have C-C, CC, O-H, C-O and C-H functional groups. The electronic structures of elements can be determined through XPS. The C1s XPS spectra of the three CQDs are shown in Fig. 3(c). Among them, the C1s peaks of CAPEI, HAPEI, and BCNPEI all contain subpeaks centered at 284.5 eV, 284.9 eV, 285.4 eV and 286.6 eV, corresponding to CC, CC, CN and CO bonds, respectively [52–54]. Furthermore, a subpeak centered at 287.3 eV, corresponding to the CO bond, appears in the C1s peak of CAPEI. A sub-peak corresponding to the CB bond at 283.9 eV is present in the C1s peak of BCNPEI. In addition, there are two sub-peaks in the B1s peak of BCNPEI, representing the bonds BN and BO, respectively (Fig. 3(d)) [55,56].

The CO groups had been considered as the photocatalytic activity center of CQDs, and it has been explained that the high upward band bending caused by it facilitates the separation of electron-hole pairs and thus contributes to the photo-catalytic efficiency [24,57]. However, among the three PEI-based CQDs prepared in our study, only CAPEI contained CO groups, and there was a significant difference in their ability to photo-degrade TC, thus surface oxygen-containing functional groups were found not to explain why BCNPEI could photo-degrade TC, but CAPEI and HAPEI could not.

In addition, the XPS spectra were analyzed in depth to explore the association between the degree of aromatization and photocatalytic activity of CAPEI, HAPEI and BCNPEI. As shown in Fig. S5(a), the carbon content of CAPEI is 61.92 %, the nitrogen content is 22.61 %, and the oxygen content is 15.48 %. HAPEI contains 64.85 % carbon, 27.91 % nitrogen, and 7.23 % oxygen (Fig. S5(b)). Amounts of carbon, nitrogen, and oxygen in BCNPEI are 29.04 %, 10.96 %, and 19.06 %, respectively, while the boron content is 40.94 % (Fig. S5(c)). Based on the ratio of subpeaks to C1s, the percentage of different bonds (CC, CC, CN, etc.) in the C1s spectrum can be obtained [58]. Thus, the percentages of different bonds in the C1s spectra of CAPEI, HAPEI and BCNPEI were calculated, as shown in Fig. S5(d). The proportions of CC in the C1s spectra of CAPEI, HAEI and BCNPEI are 15.17 %, 17.67 % and 10.49 %, respectively. According to the preliminary analysis, the higher the ratio of CC, the better the degree of conjugation of the corresponding material. The resulting band gap of the material becomes smaller, and the energy of the generated photo-generated electrons and holes is not high enough for the photocatalytic reaction. The proportions of CO bonds in the C1s spectra of CAPEI, HAEI and BCNPEI are 4.21 %, 8.17 % and 18.16 %, respectively. And the proportions of C-N in the C1s spectra of CAPEI, HAEI and BCNPEI are 13.64 %, 15.42 % and 43.48 %, respectively. In addition, the proportion of C-B bonds in BCNPEI is calculated as 10.96 %. Previous studies have shown that doping with nitrogen, oxygen or boron can theoretically weaken the degree of conjugation of the aromatized structure, thus widening the band gap of the material and even transforming it from a conductor to a semiconductor [59–61]. In our study, BCNPEI has the highest oxygen content of 19.06 %, followed by CAPEI with 15.48 % and the lowest is HAPEI with 7.23 %. BCNPEI also possesses 40.94 % of boron element. Therefore, it is speculated that this is related to their photocatalytic activity.

Then, the band gap structures of CAPEI, HAPEI and BCNPEI were investigated to probe the relationship between the energy band structure and the photodegradation properties of CQDs. As seen in Fig. 4(a), the band gap values of 1.66 eV, 1.63 eV, and 2.83 eV were calculated for CAPEI, HAPEI, and BCNPEI, respectively, based on the Tauc method [(αhυ)1/n vs. hυ] [62,63]. Apparently, there is a clear linear relationship between the order of band gap value and the photodegradation performance of CQDs for TC. Next, Mott-Schottky plots of CAPEI, HAPEI and BCNPEI were made from the capacitance values observed in the dark at 800 Hz and 1600 Hz to further determine their exact conduction band positions [39,64]. From Fig. 4(b)–(d), the conduction band (CB) values of CAPEI, HAPEI and BCNPEI are − 0.32 V and − 0.28 V and − 0.72 V vs. Ag/AgCl, respectively. Meanwhile, the positive slope in the figure represents the typical n-type semiconductor structure in CQDs [27]. Based on the CB and band gap values, their valence band (VB) values were calculated, and the corresponding band structures were plotted (Fig. 4(e)) [25]. The calculated CB values for CAPEI, HAPEI and BCNPEI are − 0.223 V and − 0.183 V and − 0.623 V, respectively, with corresponding VB values of 1.437 V and 1.447 V and 2.207 V. The oxidation potential OH-/·OH is 1.99 V, implying that only BCNPEI can produce ·OH under visible light. The reduction potential of ·O2 - is − 0.33 V. Similarly, O2 can only be reduced to ·O2 - by BCNPEI when exposed to visible light. From the above, it is concluded that the energy band structure theory well explains why BCNPEI can photodegrade TC when exposed to light, but CAPEI and HAPEI cannot.

The analysis of the experimental data suggested that the energy band structure of BCNPEI is the main driver for the effective degradation of TC by BCNPEI. To deeply understand the above results, a CQD model (Fig. S3(a)) and a BCN-CQD model (Fig. S3(b)) were constructed, and the effect of BCN heterocycle doping on the energy band structure was examined using theoretical simulations. From the experimental results, it can be seen that the band gap of BCNPEI (2.83 eV) is larger than that of CAPEI and HAPEI (1.66 eV and 1.63 eV). The band gap of the constructed CQD model is estimated to be 2.20 eV, and that of the BCN-CQD model is estimated to be 2.35 eV. Meanwhile, the VB values of the two models are calculated. The VB values of the CQD model and the BCN-CQD model are calculated as − 4.053 V and − 4.038 V, exhibiting a trend of increasing VB values. These calculated results are consistent with our experimental results that the BCNPEI has an increased band gap value and VB value compared to CAPEI and HAPEI. Although the calculated results of the model are not exactly the same as the experimental data, the consistency of the data trends still indicates that the constructed model is qualitatively reasonable. The reason for this bias is the inevitable limitation of the modeling approach, which is often seen in other studies as well [25,65].

The HOMO and LUMO orbitals of the CQD model and the BCN-CQD model are shown in Fig. S6. In the CQD model, the HOMO orbital is mainly located in the sp3 (C-C), sp2 (CC), doped nitrogen atom, and CO double bond, as well as the LUMO orbital is mainly located in the sp3 (C-C), sp2 (CC) and doped nitrogen atom. This indicates that the oxygen-containing functional groups are also part of the energy band structure (CB and VB), so the energy band structure theory can also explain the role of functional groups on the photo-catalytic activity of CQDs. Unlike the model of CQD, the HOMO orbital of the BCN-CQD model consists mainly of sp3 (C-C), sp2 (CC), doped nitrogen atom, and B-N bond, as well as the LUMO orbital consists mainly of sp3 (C-C), sp2 (CC), doped nitrogen atom, BN bond, and BC bond. Obviously, the doping of boron and nitrogen elements expands the band gap of the CQD model and enhances the photocatalytic performance of CQDs, which is also consistent with the photodegradation result.

Therefore, it is speculated that CQDs with suitable energy band structure have the ability to photo-degrade pollutants. The efficiency of CAPEI prepared with CA and PEI as precursors for photodegradation of TC in this study was only about 8 % with a band gap value of 1.66 eV. In our previous work, a CQD material named BCQD was synthesized by hydrothermal method using CA and BCN as precursors [66]. BCQD has a conduction band value of − 0.74 V, a valence band value of 1.40 V and a band gap value of 2.14 eV. Based on the energy band structure theory, BCQD can produce ·O2 - under light without ·OH. The photodegradation experiments further show that BCQD can significantly degrade TC, and ·O2 - can be detected during photodegradation. This confirms that our speculation is reasonable.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photoactive species capture experiment of BCNPEI for TC photodegradation
ce:section.ce:para: Using photoactive species capture experiments, the primary photoactive species for the photo-degradation of TC by BCNPEI were identified. The results show (Fig. 4(f)) that the photo-degradation efficiency of BCNPEI for TC is 62 % in the absence of trapping agent. By adding EDTA-2Na, the removal rate of TC decreased from 62 % to 5 %, indicating that h+ contributed significantly to the photo-degradation of TC by BCNPEI. After the addition of TB and BQ, the removal rates of TC dropped to 57 % and 54 %, respectively, indicating that ·OH and ·O2 - also participated in the process of BCNPEI degradation of TC, but compared to h+, the effect is not very obvious.

In addition, to further demonstrate the presence of photoactive species, BCNPEI was investigated using ESR spin trapping technique. The experiments were first performed under dark conditions, followed by irradiation under visible light for 2 min, 5 min and 10 min. By looking at Fig. 5(a) and (b), it can be found that when capturing ·OH and ·O2 - with DMPO reagent, the signals of DMPO-·OH and DMPO-·O2 - can be clearly detected at 2 min. And when the irradiation time increased, the signal intensity eventually grew as well. This indicates under visible light irradiation, the photocatalytic system contains both ·OH and ·O2 - active species [67]. Meanwhile, when h+ was captured with tempo reagent, it was found that the signal continuously weakened after light irradiation (Fig. 5(c)), which indicated that the sample produced h+ under light irradiation [68]. ESR tests proved the existence of h+, OH and ·O2 -, corroborating the correctness of the photoactive species capture experiment.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Possible photodegradation pathways of TC and toxicity analysis
ce:section.ce:para: In order to identify the possible degradation pathways of TC by BCNPEI, the degradation products were investigated via HPLC-MS. TC has a relative molecular mass of 455. The LC-MS spectrums of Fig. S7 show that intermediates with m/z values of 427, 410, 340, 227, and 130 were formed upon visible light irradiation. This led us to speculate on a potential pathway for the degradation of TC by BCNPEI under light ( Fig. 6(a)). Product 1 (m/z = 427) was formed by dehydration at C6 [69]. Deamidation of product 1 yielded product 2 (m/z = 410). Then, products 3 (m/z = 340), 4(m/z = 227) and 5 (m/z = 130) were yielded by stepwise de-hydroxylation, demethylation and ring opening reactions. Finally, the above products were degraded into inorganic substances such as CO2 and H2O. Similar arguments from previous studies also can be found [25].

Furthermore, Toxicity Evaluation Software Tool (T.E.S.T.) has been regularly adopted to evaluate the toxicity of photodegradation intermediates，including indicators of acute toxicity, developmental toxicity, and mutagenicity [25]. Since the toxicity of the intermediate products during photodegradation is probably greater than that of the initial pollutant, evaluating the toxicity of the intermediate products of TC photodegradation by BCNPEI is crucial for the use of BCNPEI to combat water pollution.

Fathead minnow and rats were selected to evaluate the acute toxicity of the intermediates. As shown in Fig. 6(b), the LC50-96 h (the concentration of the chemical that causes 50 % of fathead minnow mortality within 96 h) value of TC for fathead minnow was 0.90 mg/L, which was in the "highly toxic" range. For intermediates, the toxicity of products 1, 2, and 3 was greater than that of TC itself, while the toxicity of product 4 and product 5 was dramatically reduced, with LC50-96 h values shifting from the "super toxic" range to the "harmful" range. Despite the higher toxicity of the first three intermediates, it was clear that product 4 and product 5 were the major intermediates, and hence the toxicity of TC was reduced after photodegradation for fathead minnow in general. The acute toxicity of TC itself to rats was "highly toxic" (Fig. 6(c)). Except for product 3, the LD50 values of TC photodegradation intermediates exceeded the LD50 values of TC itself, especially for product 5, which indicated that most of the intermediates were less acutely toxic to rats. According to the toxicity assessment of the above TC intermediates in blackheads and rats, the acute toxicity of the photodegradation intermediates was decreased on the whole. In addition, the developmental toxicity evaluation of photodegradation intermediates was shown in Fig. 6(d). The developmental toxicity of products 1, 2, and 3 showed a decreasing trend, and the developmental toxicity of photodegradation process changed from toxicant to non-toxicant at product4 and product 5, which means that their developmental toxicity was declined. In the evaluation of the mutagenicity index of the intermediates (Fig. 6(e)), it was found that TC changed from "mutagenic positive" to "mutagenic negative" in all intermediates except product 3. Based on the above analysis, in general, the photodegradation of BCNPEI reduces the toxicity of TC. And the longer the time of light exposure, the less toxic the intermediate products in the later period are, which will be conducive to the removal of these TC photodegradation intermediates by microorganisms and plants.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Stability and recycling ability of BCNPEI
ce:section.ce:para: The stability of the photocatalysts was evaluated by FTIR spectroscopy of BCNPEI samples irradiated under a xenon lamp (equipped with a 420 nm filter) for 5 h, 10 h and 15 h. The results are shown in Fig. 5(d), and it demonstrates that the BCNPEI sample is a relatively stable material with no obvious morphological and structural changes after light exposure.

In the recycling experiment, as shown in Fig. S8, the efficiency of photodegradation of TC by BCNPEI was 62 % for the first time, 48 % for the second time, 37 % for the third time, and 33 % for the fourth time. The efficiency of BCNPEI photodegradation of TC showed a decreasing trend throughout the whole cycle, which may be due to the fact that no material regeneration was done to treat BCNPEI in this process as in the case of powder photocatalytic materials, thus the residual intermediates of photodegraded TC were adsorbed on the surface of BCNPEI and occupied the active sites. As a result, BCNPEI was not exposed to more TC and the photodegradation performance decreased.

To prove this, the interaction energies of BCN-CQD model with TC, product 3, and product 4 were calculated, as the LCMS results showed that the abundance of product 3 and product 4 remained relatively high after 180 min of photodegradation.

The molecular structure model of TC and the optimized molecular structure model of product 3 and product 4 were constructed (Fig. S9 (a)–(c)). The interaction energy between BCN-CQD model and TC is calculated to be 11.8 kcal/mol. The model shows that BCN-CQD interact with TC mainly through hydrogen bonding (Fig. S10(a)), which is due to the non-planar structure of TC that cannot interact through π-π stacking. The optimized molecular structure of product 3 can be seen that the three rings on its left side have a coplanar structure, so the quantum dot can not only interact with TC through hydrogen bonding and other ways, but also interact with product 3 through π-π stacking (Fig. S10(b)). Their interaction energy is calculated to be 23.2 kcal/mol. For product 4, due to its small molecular size, the space repulsion resistance is smaller than that of TC, hence the interaction energy of BCN-CQD and product 4 is slightly greater than that with TC, which is 18.8 kcal/mol (Fig. S10(c)). The interaction energy of BCN-CQD with product 3 and product 4 is greater than that with TC, which further confirms that BCNPEI is more inclined to adsorb intermediate products 3 and 4 during the cycle, resulting in lower photodegradation efficiency of TC. Nevertheless, BCNPEI can be found to possess a relatively stable photodegradation ability.

In this study, three PEI-based CQDs (CAPEI, HAPEI and BCNPEI) were synthesized by hydrothermal method and the structural factors that determine their performance in TC photodegradation under visible light illumination were investigated. BCNPEI has the best ability to photo-degrade TC, and the efficiency of photodegradation for TC removal reached 62 %. The suitable energy band structure enables BCNPEI to generate ·OH and ·O2 - radicals under light conditions, which make the main contribution to its photodegradation for TC. It is speculated that CQD can possess stronger photodegradation performance for organic pollutants just when it has appropriate energy band structure. In addition, BCNPEI can reduce the toxicity of TC by photodegradation, which can facilitate the subsequent biodegradation by microorganisms and plants in water. This work is helpful to further insight into the influencing mechanism of CQDs on the transformation behavior of pollutants when they are released into the water body.
